narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 72.3% chance that you would not pass, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction draws from patterns in academic records, test scores, and backgrounds of past students, focusing on factors like grades, LSAT performance, and family circumstances that reliably signal bar success. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 decile ranking of 1st, which placed you at the lower end compared to the typical 6th decile among students who passed the bar—suggesting challenges in sustaining strong performance later in your studies. Next in importance, your LSAT score of 39 actually worked in your favor, as it's above the average of about 37 for those who passed, pulling the prediction somewhat toward success. Your Year 1 decile of 2nd also contributed toward the failure outlook, again lower than the 6th decile average for passers, highlighting early academic hurdles that the model weighs heavily. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 falls short of the 3.24 average for bar passers, and your middle family income aligns with a sizable portion of successful students, these played lesser roles here. Being a full-time student matches the norm for those who pass, which is positive but not enough to offset the key concerns. Compared to admitted applicants, who typically show stronger decile rankings across years, your profile signals higher risk despite strengths like your LSAT. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model prioritizing bar passage predictors to protect program outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles, preparing rigorously for a higher LSAT if reapplying, or exploring post-baccalaureate options to strengthen your GPA. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reach out to advisors for targeted prep resources, and reapply once you've built a stronger academic trajectory. Your determination can change this story.
